evidence_quotes.quote: customers should have protections from publishers terminating video games that customers have already bought that is to say that publishers should have a end of life plan for their games that allows the game to still be accessible to consumers after support has ended.

paragraphs.text: First of all i would like to apologize for the verbosity and length of this feedback however i feel that these additions are vital if we want a strong digital environment/market...i most humbly beg for your excellencies to hear my pleas and cries. I would humbly request that customers should have protections from publishers terminating video games that customers have already bought that is to say that publishers should have a end of life plan for their games that allows the game to still be accessible to consumers after support has ended. Games are an important form of media and culture and their systematic destruction by the hands of their publishers constitutes a pervasive loss of history, culture, and the creative mark of the games creators. Though many would prefer stronger protections i am here asking simply for protections to be put in place so that these works of art won't be simply taken away from the consumer (like "the crew" was for example ) Though i would say that the code not being open-source (open to view not free to reproduce) already constitutes a privacy (could hide spyware ), security (could hide malware), and technological (makes competition harder and technological progression slower) problem for the consumer and that software has held a privileged position compared to all other works of art and engineering... Doing these end of life plans is a simple procedure that has a very small impact for the publishers but protects history, culture and most importantly the rights of EU citizens. Furthermore i would also humbly ask that it became mandatory for online stores to include SEPA transactions, direct bank transactions or other regional/secure payment methods to guarantee that our ability to pay for things not be restricted because certain credit card companies refuse to allow perfectly legal transactions. Furthermore i would also humbly ask that it became illegal for credit card providers to refuse to allow a legal transaction....Visa and Mastercard hold way above 2/3 of the credit card market and their capacity to refuse to allow a legal transaction could cause several problems such as major businesses being forced to censor themselves and their customers (such as steam and itch.io recently) On the internet there is very few ways to pay for things and credit cards constitute a de facto monopoly ( or duopoly ) and this ability to refuse could cause the collapse of the digital market...specially as we move towards a cashless credit card only society...
